text: The surgical team uses two camera trocars placed at the suprapubic and umbilical regions. The umbilical trocar provides a view of the left side and pelvis, while the suprapubic trocar is used for transverse colon and right side colon mobilization. The procedure begins with pelvic dissection, identifying and dividing the inferior mesenteric pedicle using a medial approach. The surgeon pulls the mesocolon to identify the inferior mesenteric artery from the left side, with the view from the right side showing the bifurcation for dissection.

The surgeon applies a liga clip and uses ligasure to divide the tissue distally, raising a flap while preserving retroperitoneal structures, including the ureter. The flap is raised away from the Gerota's fascia, following the embryonic vascular plane to minimize bleeding. The surgeon incises the avascular line of Toldt laterally, dissecting retrocholically to reach the splenic flexure. The gastrocolic omentum is divided, and the splenic flexure is fully mobilized. The inferior border of the pancreas is divided from the transverse colon and mesocolon, with small vessels clipped and divided.

The surgeon exposes the retroperitoneal structures, including visible vessels, within an avascular plane. Careful dissection avoids deeper planes that may contain vessels. The pelvis is approached for total mesorectal excision, preserving vascular and nerve bundles, including sympathetic and parasympathetic nerves. The dissection progresses anteriorly to the pelvic diaphragm, revealing the levator ani muscle. The right colon is mobilized by incising the peritoneum below the cecum and advancing towards the hepatic flexure, identifying and preserving the duodenum. A retrocolic tunnel is created, and the vascular pedicle, including the middle and right colic vessels, is skeletonized and divided.

The surgeon manipulates the hepatic flexure of the colon using a cartilaginous maneuver to expose the duodenum and head of the pancreas, revealing the middle colic vessels. These vessels are approached supracolically, clipped, and divided. The terminal ileum is divided with an endocutter, freeing the specimen. Retracting stitches are used for intrasphincteric dissection, and the internal sphincter is divided. The colon and rectum, along with a portion of the terminal ileum, are removed. An intracorporeal pouch is created using two firings of a 60mm stapler, forming a 12cm pouch that is anastomosed to the external sphincter, completing a coloanal anastomosis. A covering ileostomy and drain are placed to conclude the procedure.
